findings.description: Stored XSS in NotificationMessage - entity names/descriptions flow into HTML notification without encoding
findings.reasoning: The NotificationMessage class explicitly calls setHtmlContentAllowed(true), which means the description passed to it is rendered as HTML. The description string is built from entity names (distribution set names, target names, rollout names) retrieved from the database without any HTML encoding. The dataflow shows entity names (from getName() on JPA entities) flow through VaadinMessageSource.getMessage() message formatting, through UINotification, into NotificationMessage.setDescription() which is then rendered as HTML. An attacker who can create entities with names containing HTML/JavaScript (e.g., a distribution set named `<script>alert(1)</script>`) can trigger XSS in any user's browser when they view the notification.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Entity getName() -> VaadinMessageSource.getMessage() -> UINotification -> NotificationMessage.setDescription() -> rendered as HTML via setHtmlContentAllowed(true)
evidence.notes: The setHtmlContentAllowed(true) call explicitly opts into HTML rendering, making any unsanitized entity data in the description injectable.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates an entity (e.g. distribution set) with a name containing HTML/JS payload; when the entity is saved or modified, a notification is shown to UI users with the entity name rendered as HTML
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs write access to create or modify named entities (distribution sets, targets, rollouts) in the hawkBit system

findings.description: Stored XSS in HawkbitCommonUtil.getBoldHTMLText - entity names inserted directly into HTML bold tags without encoding
findings.reasoning: The getBoldHTMLText() method wraps the input text in HTML bold tags without any HTML encoding. This text is then used in getFormatedLabel() (line 249) which creates a Label with ContentMode.HTML, and in getSoftwareModuleName() (line 153) which is set on captions with ContentMode.HTML (AbstractTableDetailsLayout line 218). Entity names from the database (software module names, distribution set names) flow through this function without encoding and are rendered as HTML. An attacker who can create or name an entity with HTML/JS content can inject and execute scripts.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Entity getName() -> HawkbitCommonUtil.getBoldHTMLText() -> getSoftwareModuleName() -> Label.setValue() with ContentMode.HTML -> XSS

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a software module with a malicious name; when any user views the module's detail panel, the name is rendered unescaped as HTML in the header caption
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs permission to create/rename software modules in hawkBit

findings.description: Stored XSS in AbstractFilterButtons.prepareFilterButtonCaption - tag/type names inserted into HTML button caption without encoding
findings.reasoning: The prepareFilterButtonCaption() method builds an HTML string by appending entity names (tag/type names from the database) directly without HTML encoding. The `name` parameter contains the tag name from the database. The resulting HTML string is set as the caption of a Vaadin Button, which renders captions as HTML by default. An attacker who can create a tag or type with a name containing HTML/JavaScript can inject malicious content that executes when any user views the filter button panel.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Tag/type entity getName() -> prepareFilterButtonCaption(name, color) -> Button.setCaption(html) -> Vaadin renders HTML caption
evidence.notes: Vaadin Button captions are rendered as HTML when the caption contains HTML tags

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a distribution or target tag with malicious HTML in its name; the filter button panel renders the tag name as unescaped HTML in button captions visible to all users
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs write permission to create/rename tags or types in hawkBit

findings.description: Stored XSS in DistributionSetDetails - software module names inserted into HTML strings without encoding and rendered via ContentMode.HTML label
findings.reasoning: The updateSoftwareModule() method in DistributionSetDetails builds HTML strings by directly appending software module names and versions (from database entities) into HTML italic tags without encoding. The result is set on a Label via createSoftwareModuleLayout() which uses HawkbitCommonUtil.getFormatedLabel() that creates a Label with ContentMode.HTML. Any attacker who can create a software module with HTML content in its name or version can inject XSS payloads that execute when users view the distribution set details.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: SoftwareModule.getName()/getVersion() -> HTML string concatenation with <I> tags -> Label.setValue() -> ContentMode.HTML rendering
evidence.notes: getFormatedLabel() confirmed to create Label(content, ContentMode.HTML) at HawkbitCommonUtil line 249

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a software module with malicious HTML in its name/version; when users view a distribution set with this module assigned, the XSS payload executes in the details panel
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs write access to create software modules in hawkBit

findings.description: Stored XSS in HawkbitCommonUtil.createAssignmentMessage - entity names from database interpolated into HTML message string without encoding
findings.reasoning:
The createAssignmentMessage() method builds HTML strings by passing entity names (target names, tag names from the database) as arguments to i18n.getMessage(), which formats them into message strings that are then concatenated with literal HTML (`
` tags). The resulting string is passed to UINotification which passes it to NotificationMessage.setDescription() where setHtmlContentAllowed(true) is called. Entity names are never HTML-encoded before being interpolated into these messages.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: NamedEntity.getName() -> i18n.getMessage() args -> formMsg StringBuilder -> UINotification -> NotificationMessage.setDescription() -> setHtmlContentAllowed(true)
evidence.notes:
The
appending confirms this is treated as HTML. Entity names are user-controlled via the REST API.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a tag with an XSS payload in its name; when any user assigns a target to this tag, the notification message containing the unencoded tag name executes script in the user's browser
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs write permission to create tags in hawkBit

findings.description: Stored XSS in HawkbitCommonUtil.getArtifactoryDetailsLabelId - software module names inserted into HTML caption without encoding
findings.reasoning: The getArtifactoryDetailsLabelId() method calls getBoldHTMLText(name) with the software module name from the database, wrapping it in `<b>` tags without HTML encoding. The result is wrapped in getCaptionText() which adds more HTML div tags, then used in ArtifactDetailsLayout where the label has ContentMode.HTML set (confirmed at line 168 and 433 of ArtifactDetailsLayout.java). A software module named with HTML/JS content would execute in the browser.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: SoftwareModule.getName() -> getArtifactoryDetailsLabelId() -> getBoldHTMLText() (no encoding) -> getCaptionText() -> Label with ContentMode.HTML
evidence.notes: ContentMode.HTML confirmed at ArtifactDetailsLayout lines 168 and 433

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a software module with a malicious name; when any user views the Artifact Details panel for that module, the unencoded name is rendered as HTML in the panel header, executing the XSS payload
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs permission to create/rename software modules in hawkBit

findings.description: Stored XSS in SoftwareModuleDetailsTable - SW module names inserted into HTML string and rendered via ContentMode.HTML label
findings.reasoning: The createSoftModuleLayout() in SoftwareModuleDetailsTable calls HawkbitCommonUtil.getFormatedLabel("") which creates a Label with ContentMode.HTML (confirmed at HawkbitCommonUtil line 249). It then calls softwareModule.setValue(softwareModNameVersion) where softwareModNameVersion is built from sw.getName() + ":" + sw.getVersion() without HTML encoding. Since the label uses ContentMode.HTML, any HTML in the name or version will be rendered as HTML and scripts will execute.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: SoftwareModule.getName()/getVersion() -> getFormattedNameVersion() (no encoding) -> Label.setValue() -> ContentMode.HTML rendering

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a software module with XSS in its version string; when users view a distribution set's software module details table, the payload executes in the details panel
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs write access to create/update software modules in hawkBit

findings.description: Stored XSS in TargetBulkUpdateWindowLayout - i18n-formatted count label with HTML tags set on Vaadin label caption that renders HTML
findings.reasoning: The getFormattedCountLabelValue() method builds an HTML string with literal HTML tags (br, font). While the count values here are integers (less risky), the overall HTML construction pattern shows the label accepts HTML. More critically, this is set via setCaption() on targetsCountLabel. The caption is rendered HTML in Vaadin when it contains HTML tags. More concerning is that in other callers within this file (line 343, 346), the label is built with potentially user-controlled data. The HTML construction pattern with setCaption() is present throughout this class.

evidence.dataflow_analysis:
HTML construction with
tags -> Label.setCaption() -> rendered as HTML in Vaadin
evidence.notes: While counts are integers here, the broader pattern shows HTML rendering. This confirms the HTML rendering path for this label.

exploit_payload.scenario: HTML is constructed with literal tags and set as a Vaadin label caption that renders HTML, establishing an XSS-capable rendering path for bulk upload result messages
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs to trigger bulk upload completion flow

findings.description: Stored XSS in HawkbitCommonUtil.getSoftwareModuleName - entity name/caption directly concatenated into HTML div without encoding, used in HTML-mode label
findings.reasoning: getSoftwareModuleName() directly concatenates the `caption` and `name` parameters (entity names from the database) into an HTML string with DIV_DESCRIPTION_START/END wrappers and getBoldHTMLText() wrapping without any HTML encoding. This is called from AbstractTableDetailsLayout.setName() (line 142) where the result is set on `caption` label that has ContentMode.HTML set (line 218). Both the header caption (software module type name) and the value (software module name) are unencoded. An attacker who controls either can inject XSS.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Entity getName() -> getSoftwareModuleName() (no encoding) -> Label.setValue() with ContentMode.HTML -> XSS
evidence.notes:
DIV_DESCRIPTION_START = '

'; both caption and name are concatenated without escaping

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a software module type or module with malicious HTML in its name; when any user views the details panel for an entity of that type, the unencoded name executes script via the HTML-mode caption label
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs write permission to create/rename software module types or modules in hawkBit

findings.description: Stored XSS via entity name in getSoftwareModuleName - unencoded name concatenated into HTML string set on Label with ContentMode.HTML
findings.reasoning: The `getSoftwareModuleName` method directly concatenates the `name` parameter into HTML markup without any HTML encoding. The `name` value comes from entity names (Distribution Set name, Software Module name, Target name) stored in the database - these can be set by users or IoT devices via the REST API. The returned HTML string is passed to `caption.setValue(...)` at AbstractTableDetailsLayout.java line 142, and the `caption` Label has `ContentMode.HTML` explicitly set (AbstractTableDetailsLayout.java line 226: `caption.setContentMode(ContentMode.HTML)`). This means Vaadin renders the string as raw HTML, executing any injected scripts. The flow is: 1. Attacker creates/renames a Distribution Set, Software Module, or Target with a name like `` via REST API 2. Name is stored in the database 3. When a user views the details panel, `populateData()` calls `setName(getDefaultCaption(), getName())` (AbstractTableDetailsLayout.java line 282) 4. `setName` calls `HawkbitCommonUtil.getSoftwareModuleName(headerCaption, value)` which concatenates without encoding 5. The result is set via `caption.setValue(...)` on a Label with `ContentMode.HTML` 6. Vaadin renders it as HTML, executing the injected script for any authenticated user viewing the panel

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Entity name (from DB, set by attacker via REST API) -> getName() -> setName() -> getSoftwareModuleName() -> caption.setValue() -> Label with ContentMode.HTML -> XSS execution in browser

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates or renames an IoT entity (Distribution Set, Software Module, or Target) with an XSS payload in the name field via REST API; when any admin views the details panel, the payload executes in their browser
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs write access to the hawkBit REST API to create/rename entities; admin must view the details panel of the malicious entity

findings.description: Stored XSS via entity name in createAssignmentMessage - unencoded entity names concatenated into HTML notification message rendered with setHtmlContentAllowed(true)
findings.reasoning:
The `createAssignmentMessage` method builds HTML notification messages by calling `i18n.getMessage(...)` with entity names (from `result.getAssignedEntity().get(0).getName()` and `result.getUnassignedEntity().get(0).getName()`) and `tagName` as message format arguments. These names are stored in the database and can be set by attackers via the REST API.

The resulting HTML string is passed to `notification.displaySuccess(...)` (TargetTable.java:564, DistributionTable.java:381, DistributionTagDropEvent.java:146), which calls `UINotification.displaySuccess()` → `NotificationMessage.showNotification()` → `decorate()` which calls `setHtmlContentAllowed(true)` (NotificationMessage.java line 64).

With HTML rendering enabled on Vaadin's `Notification`, any HTML/JavaScript in entity names will be executed in the browser of the user performing the tag assignment operation.

The message template `message.target.assigned.one` takes the entity name as a format argument, so the entity name is embedded directly in the rendered HTML without encoding.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Entity name (from DB, set by attacker) -> result.getAssignedEntity().get(0).getName() -> i18n.getMessage() arg -> HTML string -> displaySuccess() -> NotificationMessage.setHtmlContentAllowed(true) -> XSS in browser

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker registers an IoT device (target) with an XSS payload as the device name; when an admin assigns a tag to that target in the management UI, the notification popup executes the payload in the admin's browser
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs write access to the hawkBit REST API to register a device with a malicious name; an admin must perform a tag assignment involving that device

findings.description: Stored XSS via tagName and entity names in createAssignmentMessage - multiple unencoded user-controlled strings concatenated into HTML notification rendered with setHtmlContentAllowed(true)
findings.reasoning:
Lines 306, 310, 314-315, and 317 in `createAssignmentMessage` all use `tagName` (a user-controlled tag name from the database) and/or entity names as i18n message arguments. These are appended into an HTML string without HTML encoding. The `tagName` comes from `DistributionTagDropEvent`, `TargetTable`, and `DistributionTable` callers - all from database-stored tag names that users can create via the REST API or UI.

The returned HTML string flows into `notification.displaySuccess()` → `NotificationMessage.setHtmlContentAllowed(true)`, rendering all of these injected values as raw HTML in the notification popup visible to the user performing the operation. An attacker who can create a tag with an XSS payload name can trigger script execution when any user performs a tag assignment involving that tag.

evidence.code_context:
Lines 306-317 of HawkbitCommonUtil.java: tagName used as i18n.getMessage arg in 'message.target.assigned.many', 'message.target.unassigned.one', 'message.target.unassigned.many' result.getUnassignedEntity().get(0).getName() used in 'message.target.unassigned.one' All appended to formMsg StringBuilder with '
' HTML tags NotificationMessage.java:64: setHtmlContentAllowed(true); Callers pass tagName from: TargetTable.java:564 -> targTagName (target tag name from DB) DistributionTable.java:381 -> distTagName (distribution tag name from DB) DistributionTagDropEvent.java:146 -> distTagName (distribution tag name from DB)
evidence.dataflow_analysis: tagName (attacker-controlled, from DB) -> i18n.getMessage() arg -> HTML string -> displaySuccess() -> setHtmlContentAllowed(true) -> XSS

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a tag (distribution or target) with an XSS payload in its name; when an admin performs a bulk tag assignment involving that tag, the success notification executes the payload in the admin's browser
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs write access to hawkBit REST API to create a tag with malicious name; admin must perform a tag assignment that triggers createAssignmentMessage with assignedCount > 1 or unassignedCount pathways

findings.description: Stored XSS in createAssignmentMessage at lines 310 and 314-315 - unencoded entity names and tagName in HTML notification with setHtmlContentAllowed(true)
findings.reasoning: Lines 310 and 314-315 in `createAssignmentMessage` embed `result.getUnassignedEntity().get(0).getName()` (entity name) and `tagName` directly as arguments to i18n message templates for the unassign case. These values are stored in the database and can be crafted by an attacker via the REST API. The HTML string is returned and passed to `displaySuccess()` which uses `setHtmlContentAllowed(true)`, enabling XSS execution in the notification popup. This covers the unassignment code paths (lines 310-317) as separate identified sinks by CodeQL at lines 310 and 314-315.

  tagName at line 312 and 315 (single and bulk unassign)
  All appended to HTML formMsg without encoding

Callers: TargetTable.java:564, DistributionTable.java:381, DistributionTagDropEvent.java:146
evidence.dataflow_analysis: Entity name and tagName (attacker-controlled, from DB) -> i18n.getMessage() format args -> HTML string -> displaySuccess() -> setHtmlContentAllowed(true) -> XSS

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates a tag with SVG/event-handler XSS payload as name; admin unassigning a single target from that tag triggers the payload via the success notification
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker needs write access to hawkBit REST API to create tag with malicious name; admin must perform an unassignment operation involving that tag
